blocks.content:
In previous topics, we already used conditions with `if`, `else`, and `else if`.

Now we will focus on comparison operators.

Comparison operators are used inside conditions to compare values.

There are several comparison operators:

`>` greater than
`<` less than
`>=` greater than or equal to
`<=` less than or equal to
`==` equal to
`!=` not equal to

In this topic, we will focus on the operator `==`.

It is used to check if two values are equal.

Beginners often confuse `=` and `==`.

`=` assigns a value.

`==` compares values.
Let’s look at how this code works.
After `int temperature = 0;`, the variable `temperature` contains the value 0.

In the first `if`, the operator `>` is used:

`if (temperature > 0)`

The program checks whether 0 is greater than 0.

This is false, so the program skips this block.

In the `else if`, the operator `==` is used:

`else if (temperature == 0)`

The program checks whether the value is equal to 0.

This condition is true, so the program executes this block.

After finding the first true condition, the program stops checking the rest of the chain.

The `else` block does not run.

Retype this code and check it.

Then change the value of the variable `temperature`.

Try:

5
0
-3

After each change, press Check and see how the result changes.

Create a similar program.

Create a variable `points` and set it to 10.

If `points` is greater than 10, print:

"More than 10"

If `points` is equal to 10, print:

"Exactly 10"

Otherwise print:

"Less than 10"

In this topic, we looked at one comparison operator: `==`.

Other comparison operators (`>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `!=`) work in the same way.

They compare values and return either `true` or `false`.

The program then uses this result to decide which code to run.
